question_text:
Copy the following on a dot grid. For each figure draw two more lines to make a shape that has a line of symmetry. 9.2 Rotational Symmetry
The Windmill in the picture looks paper symmetrical but there is no line of symmetry!
However, if you fold it, the two halves will not exactly overlap. On the other hand, if you rotate it by 90° about the red point at the centre, the Windmill looks exactly the same.
We say that the Windmill has rotational symmetry.
When talking
of rotational there is a symmetry, always a fixed point about which the object is rotated. This fixed point is called the centre of rotation.
Will the Windmill above l0ok exactly the same when rotated through
1 an angle ofless than 90?
No!
An angle through which a figure can be rotated to look exactly the same is called an angle of rotational symmetry, or just an angle of symmetry, for short.
subject: Mathematics

question_text:
Draw a 6 by 6 grid. Two players take turns covering two adjacent squares by drawing a line. The line can be placed either way: horizontally or vertically. The lines cannot overlap. The game goes on till a player is not able to place any more lines. The player who is not able to place a line loses.
With what strategy play to win this can one game?
Y U
R
When a figure is made up of parts that repeat in a definite pattern, that the figure has symmetry. We say t wesay that such a figure is symmetrical.
A line that cuts aj plane figure into two
parts that exactly overlap when folded along g that line is called a line of symmetry
or axis of symmetry of the figure.
A figure may have multiple lines of s symmetry.
Sometimes a figure looks exactly the same when it is rotated by an of the figure. A figure that has an angle of symmetry strictly between
0 and 360 degrees is said to have rotational symmetry. The point of the figure about which the rotation occurs is called the centre of rotation.
A figure may have multiple e angles of symmetry.
figures may have a line of symmetry Some but no angle of symmetry, while others may have angles of symmetry but no lines of symmetry. Some figures may have both lines of symmetry as well as angles of symmetry.
subject: Mathematics
